title: Dead Throne: World of Veles - a casual unboxing
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x3gjYoDR5tA

Daniel (@dungeondive) unboxes the second edition of Dead Throne: The World of Veles, an open-world sandbox overland adventure — he skipped the first edition and its odd self-automating mechanical marketplace, and is relieved this version drops the gimmick. The box is card-heavy: 380 cards, 150 magical scrolls that act like a hand of playable powers (lightning strikes, defensive boosts, shunting enemies around the board), and region decks for woods, rocks, fields and docks stuffed with enemies, choose-your-own-choice quest cards, and reward-bearing hunts. He's charmed by the bold, cartoony, almost amateurish art and an anachronistic feel — as if the game fell out of a time warp, ignoring 15 years of design trends — comparing the vibe to Return of the Heroes. Standees (he's glad he skipped the minis), big double-sided hex tiles, and wooden components abound, though he's openly worried the flimsy market insert will fall apart. His main disappointment is that the scenarios, including the expansion pack's, skew heavily competitive rather than co-op or solo — though he plans to play most two-handed. A game he thinks could go either way, but whose sandbox promise genuinely intrigues him.

----

Dead Throne feels, to Daniel, like it 'fell out of a time warp,' ignoring modern design trends. Is there charm in a game that plays like it's from another era, or do you want your hobby always moving forward?
